title: Kinetically constrained superradiance
authors: Luis Fernando dos Prazeres, Hossein Hosseinabadi, Jamir Marino
abstract: We introduce kinetically constrained superradiance, a form of cooperative emission in which interactions imprint configuration-dependent energy shifts on optical transitions, splitting Dicke superradiance into multiple, frequency-resolved collective decay channels. Each channel selectively radiates from distinct many-body spin configurations, generating a hierarchy of dissipative time scales and sequential relaxation dynamics. Unlike conventional superradiance, where permutation symmetry enforces relaxation to a trivial steady state, configuration-selective emission can trap finite-momentum spin-wave excitations and stabilize long-lived entanglement. Remarkably, these correlations are generated purely by dissipation in the absence of entangling coherent dynamics. Our results point to modern superradiant experiments as scalable resources for dissipative engineering of correlated quantum states.
summary: This paper introduces 'kinetically constrained superradiance,' a phenomenon where atomic interactions split superradiant decay into multiple frequency-dependent channels. This mechanism breaks permutation symmetry, allowing the system to bypass the trivial steady state of standard Dicke superradiance and instead stabilize long-lived, entangled many-body states. It demonstrates that dissipation alone can be used as a resource for engineering correlated quantum states.
main_problem: How to overcome the limitation of conventional Dicke superradiance, where permutation symmetry leads to a trivial steady state, by using configuration-dependent energy shifts to engineer long-lived entanglement and trapped excitations.
main_result: The discovery of kinetically constrained superradiance, where interactions split superradiance into multiple frequency-resolved decay channels, allowing the system to reach a non-trivial steady state with stabilized, long-lived entanglement and trapped excitations.
method: The authors use a Lindblad master equation formalism derived via the Born-Markov and Redfield approximations, supplemented by stochastic quantum trajectory simulations to study entanglement and steady-state properties.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional array of N two-level atoms with nearest-neighbor interactions (Hamiltonian includes a transition energy delta and interaction strength J) coupled to a vacuum electromagnetic field in a subwavelength arrangement.
key_observables: Frequency-resolved emission rates (I_xi), total emission intensity, peak intensity, burst width, delay time, steady-state excitation density, and half-chain von Neumann entropy.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (J), transition energy (delta), number of atoms (N), and the regime of strong interaction (J >= delta) versus weak interaction (J << delta).
main_assumptions: Subwavelength arrangement of atoms (d << lambda), Born-Markov approximation, and the use of the Rotating-Wave/Secular approximation when interaction-induced splittings are large.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of collective coupling and conditional spin-flip processes; Figure 4 compares entanglement entropy evolution between constrained and Dicke superradiance; Supplementary figures show frequency-resolved emission components, scaling of burst parameters, and steady-state excitation density.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of kinetically constrained superradiance, provides a microscopic derivation of the frequency-resolved Lindblad master equation, analyzes the resulting sequential relaxation dynamics and scaling laws, and concludes with an investigation of steady-state entanglement and excitation trapping.
why_interesting: It presents a novel mechanism for dissipative state engineering where purely dissipative dynamics (without coherent driving) can generate and stabilize complex, many-body entangled states, which is highly relevant for quantum memory and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Panel (a) schematically depicts the regime of collective coupling, where atoms are arranged on distances much smaller than the the relevant transition wavelengths of the constrained emission processes (d ≪λξ). Panels (b)–(d) illustrate the conditional spin-flip processes (with associated decay rates γξ) selected by the local projectors P ξ j for ξ = 2, 1, 0; these correspond to emission events conditioned on the number of excited nearest neighbors, ξ.

Figure 2. Superradiant bursts. Left panel (a): Emission in- tensity for J/∆= 0.2, showing the frequency-resolved chan- nels ξ = 0, 1, 2 and the total intensity, together with Dicke superradiance for reference. Right panel (b): Strongly inter- acting regime (J/∆= 1) in different time units. The main panel is plotted on the slowest time units (γ−1 0 ). Insets: In- tensities in two different time units, γ−1 2 (upper inset), and γ−1 1 (bottom inset), highlighting the hierarchy γ2 ≫γ1 ≫γ0 and the metastable plateau (bottom inset) before the decay (main panel).

Figure 4. Trajectory-level entanglement entropy. Main panel: Time evolution of the entanglement entropy of half of the system for N = 10, averaged over individual quantum-jump trajectories in the weak interacting regime (J/∆= 0.2). In- set: Distribution of the steady-state entanglement entropy obtained from 103 trajectories.
Figure 3. Excitation dynamics. Dynamical densities nk(t)/n(t) for the modes k = 0 and k = π. Inset: dynam- ics of nk(t) for all momenta in a chain of N = 12 spins with J/∆= 0.2.
